top_arguments.body:
Just swallow your pride and ask to go back.

If you left on good terms, the following are very likely:

1. They are still in the period where they wish they had you back
2. Any halfway decent leader would understand if their employee felt they had an opportunity to chase after a better career. They also understand when things don't work out.
3. No way they filled the position yet.
top_arguments.author: wirsteve

top_arguments.body:
I was in the ICU but was improving drastically and wanted to get ahead on some stuff, so I joined a Teams call on my phone with the camera off. 

My boss's boss was very much a camera-on lady who was terrified of people OEing, so she called me out to turn my camera on. 

Ok, here I am. In a hospital bed, IV bags in full view, adhesive residue on my face from where they had cannulas affixed in place, etc. I looked like death, but felt well enough to keep going. She asked me where I was. 

"ICU"
top_arguments.author: bolivar-shagnasty

top_arguments.body: Location doesn't matter though, it's illegal not to pay you for working
top_arguments.author: floraster

top_arguments.author: Slorgasm

top_arguments.body:
I was once scolded into putting my camera back on during a long meeting. My direct report got told by her VP that I needed to turn it back on. 
I explained to my manager why I had briefly turned it off. 

I guess in your case, if they generally want cameras on, I would have reached out to whoever you report to, assuming they’re on the meeting, and explained that if needed I can turn my camera on but that I have a migraine and was looking very ill. 

There is the chance LDI that they may tell you

top_arguments.body:
Yes - OP, leave the 3 week job off your resume entirely. It never happened as far as your work history and future interviews are concerned.

You have a perfectly good explanation why you left your 7 year job: you got along great with everyone at your old job. You did great work. You will be happy for them to reach out to your references. 

But you left because you found that you missed collaborating with people in person. 

They'll eat that shit up. In-person employers LOVE to hear that.

In general, do it if you can. Just understand that it is discretionary, not obligatory.

What I would recommend is that you give some kind of notice, if you can help it.

Even one day of written notice, or "effective immediately" is better than flat out ghosting.

1.topic_label: Entry-Level Job Search and Career Starts

top_arguments.body:
1. There's no shame in doing what you have to do to get by.

2. You are more than your job.

I didn't even break into the industry I built my career in until my late twenties, and that ball didn't truly start rolling until mid thirties. You have lots of time to get into your groove. And no matter how long it takes or where life takes you, you don't have to be embarrassed or ashamed. The right people in your life will never look down on you for doing your best.
top_arguments.author: Apprehensive_Low3600

top_arguments.body: It could also be a sign of poor ergonomics or failure to rest your eyes during the day, performing high-focus, cognitively taxing work or having to communicate a lot while being an introvert. :)
top_arguments.author: FRELNCER

top_arguments.body: Don’t let anyone tell you that you’ve made a bad choice. You have mouths to feed and you should be commended for your humility. And I have a hunch it will end up great for you.
top_arguments.author: MarkAmericaSmith

top_arguments.body:
oh man, my friend had his “fuck this, i’m out” moment too. his boss literally said in a meeting that “weekends are a mindset.” he didn’t rage quit on the spot but he knew right then he was done. honestly, reading your post, i can’t help but wonder if that meeting was just the trigger for you and the real decision had been building for months. have you thought about when you first started feeling that burnout? sometimes we don’t notice until something ridiculous tips it over the edge.

top_arguments.body:
Yes, salary transparency helps employees, always. Salary secrecy only helps employers. 

Would you rather help some rich dude in a c-suite office, or a fellow working class individual?
top_arguments.author: Cautious_Midnight_67

top_arguments.body: Sounds like you’re about to be given a heavier workload, but it’s great to hear that they’re prepping it by also increasing your pay. Congrats!
top_arguments.author: geehaad11

top_arguments.body: I’d email them back and approach it from the “clerical error” route of “oh hey, I was reviewing my offer letter and noticed an error on the pay. Can you please update it to the correct 20.50/hr? Can’t wait to start!” Then wait. If they come back with anything other than “oh we’re sorry, here’s your updated version” then they’re playing games and not worth your time.
top_arguments.author: Senior-Pineapple-177

top_arguments.body: Cool, now job hop for another 30%.
top_arguments.author: FlashCrashBash

top_arguments.body:
You applied for the new job for more than just the money. If the new job was only slightly higher salary but the same job, you wouldn’t have applied. Adding “interest” to what you do each day is way more valuable than a few dollars. I would recommend you go to the next place even if it was a slight pay cut but it isn’t, you’re getting a raise from where you are now. 

As for the current job, you should never, ever, renegotiate after putting in your time. The boss you have may not have any say wh

top_arguments.body: A Sunday night interview is literally insane!!

First raise in 2.5 years?? Your sign to leave was 1.5 years ago. If you're not getting adjustments every year, you're being screwed. No matter how cool your coworkers are or how much you like your job. 

You owe it to future you to start looking!

I recommend listening and offering as little information as possible. 

You are leaving your current role for a reason so unless they offer you something you can’t say no to, I’d stick to your guns.
top_arguments.author: PossessionKooky3848

top_arguments.body:
Hunt for a new job, and if you find one that works for you, leave for it. 

If you have to argue for raises and opportunities, the business is complacent and doesn’t value you. 

And don’t work with the business on counteroffers, as it seldom works to the employee’s benefit. Just give them a resignation date and don’t discuss it, other than handover.

top_arguments.body:
The laws changed recently to say that jobs must post salary ranges in most states, so companies are ‘following the law’ by posting wildly large ranges. It’s definitely against the spirit of the law, if not against the law itself.

Before this, often no ranges were posted at all. Tbh, I don’t know if that was better or worse. 

I’m surprised they kept going with you if you were actually out of their REAL range. That sucks, I’m sorry.

top_arguments.body:
Yes submit the earnings with 0 earnings. Hire right will verify it because it's legit and you'll be fine. They don't care you made money.  Your instructions from hire right probably tell you to block out financial information anyway so just black out the earnings sections and call it a day.  They only want to see your name, and address on a company letterhead.

Hire right only exists to verify info you put on your resume.  They don't really care what you made.

top_arguments.body: Don’t burn bridges. Continue to be cordial and take the meeting, then decide if you want to move forward. Just let them know you have other commitments that might come up
top_arguments.author: FrankRSavage

top_arguments.body: This! I once worked with a brilliant woman who was almost sabotaged by a reference. Fortunately the person who hired her informed her that her old boss gave a horrible reference. I happened to know her old boss and knew he was a bitter jealous man.
top_arguments.author: hrdbeinggreen

top_arguments.body: It's time to give a different reference.
top_arguments.author: ailish

top_arguments.body: "I was just informed that my previous reference has a personal emergency to attend to and will be unavailable. Please context \[new reference\] who can also speak in depth to my experience"
top_arguments.author: _autumnwhimsy

top_arguments.body: They want the contact info for 3 references. Just bring a printed piece of paper that looks cleanly-formatted with your references' names and contact information.
top_arguments.author: natewOw

top_arguments.body: The way I read this was to physically bring the people with you and you know what? I think thats a powermove
top_arguments.author: MurkyConnectionB

top_arguments.body: While true, the way they worded it could also mean you need to bring those people with you. Which as ridiculous as it is, is kinda hilarious. Unless they actually did want the references to come in person at the time of interview, then it'd be severely unprofessional.
top_arguments.author: Traditional-Handle83

top_arguments.body: It would so fun to show up with 3 actual people at the interview
top_arguments.author: NorCalMikey

top_arguments.body:
You asked them to be a reference.

It sounds like they did reference on your behalf.

They were also asked to apply, and did not decline.

They were selected.

 

 
I can understand you feeling some way about them not declining the invitation to apply, but that would merely have been a courtesy on their part, not an obligation.

When I first saw your title, I thought that instead of being your reference, they simply took the job themself. That would be been slimy. But, what they did does not app
top_arguments.author: BrainWaveCC

top_arguments.body: Entirely normal. Companies have no obligation to give references (or referees) at all.
top_arguments.author: malicious_joy42

top_arguments.body: In person in an interview? I can’t say I’ve been asked but on pretty much every application, yes. But yeah you should probably be more encouraging to your wife
top_arguments.author: Lurkario-

top_arguments.body: Yes. And they have all been called. My last four jobs.
top_arguments.author: Imsortofok

top_arguments.body: i had a manager like this. Definitely agree with u on that
top_arguments.author: Star_Fall05

top_arguments.body: Or if the manager is friendly with a coworker that you still keep in touch with and still works there. They will try to get info out of that coworker. Or if that coworker pretends to be your friend and out of nowhere after many months ask you for a life update when they really snooping around for info to see how you've been doing since you left

13.reference_frame: Topic focus: References and Reference Checks. Representative posts: PSA: When leaving a job, do not give them any ideas of where you’re heading | My Reference Took My Job and now wants to Collaborate?! | Lied in the Interview, they now want references
13.dominant_position: PSA: When leaving a job, do not give them any ideas of where you’re heading

reference_posts.title: PSA: When leaving a job, do not give them any ideas of where you’re heading
My Reference Took My Job and now wants to Collaborate?!
Lied in the Interview, they now want references

top_arguments.body: Buddy of mine i worked with, I swear he was on the longest pip i've ever seen. Went on almost 3 years. Kept getting extended
top_arguments.author: CrownstrikeIntern

top_arguments.body: Update your resume’ immediately and start applying to jobs. They are going to get rid of you, I’m sorry but I’ve seen this same movie before.
top_arguments.author: Mundane_Position79

top_arguments.body: Hell yes. Now you should be looking for that next job. PIP=paid interview period.
top_arguments.author: Historical_Oven7806

top_arguments.body: Yeah, kind of. You just need to be more careful about what you share at work. Keep job or transfer talks outside company channels. Learn from it and move on.
top_arguments.author: SimilarAd2705

top_arguments.body: It is rare to survive a PIP. The target is still on your back though.
top_arguments.author: Better_North3957

top_arguments.body: Yes, your colleagues aren’t your friends…
top_arguments.author: parrexcellence

top_arguments.body: Never send job stuff on work emails or communication apps.... My brother works IT. He told me most companies flagged web pages and emails where set for job searching. Not porn site
top_arguments.author: robertva1

top_arguments.body: I'm just here to say I work in HR and this is screaming all sorts of red flags to me. Document everything. Keep a strong paper trail.
top_arguments.author: karatekirby

top_arguments.body: Lmao that’s just employee abuse
top_arguments.author: SS324

top_arguments.body:
PIPs are usually a set up to let someone go. They are very rarely a plan to help someone improve their performance.

With that said, come up with a way to discuss the ending of your employment in an interview that highlights your strengths and your eagerness to carry forward. 

People bounce back all the time from PIPs and worse.
top_arguments.author: onions-make-me-cry

top_arguments.body: If you're in the US, I would highly recommend filing a complaint with the EEOC and your state FEPA (fair employment practices agency).
top_arguments.author: _NightmareKingGrimm_

top_arguments.body: I’d make a point to point out the fact that you hit what you needed to hit. Boss sounds like an asshole.
top_arguments.author: SpicySquirt

top_arguments.body: My managers did this to me after I informed them of my intention to be a living donor to my father-in-law. Recovery was going to be an ~10 week process, they were upset with the amount of time I’d be off. I had great reviews before this but I knew they were also unhappy to work more hours with London overlap. I knew they wanted to make a change and this is the justification they needed. So I called them out, got an employment lawyer involved. Best thing to do is make sure anything they say is do
top_arguments.author: PerBnb

top_arguments.body:
My old manager was one of the most tireless and dependable people I have ever worked with. We were all confused when he was placed on PIP. About a month later his position was replaced entirely by “promoting” three technicians to take over his duties. With no raises. 

I can’t even wrap my head around how fucked we all are.

reference_posts.title: My PIP ends today
I was asked to quit today.
If you are placed on a PIP, start looking for a new job IMMEDIATELY

top_arguments.body: They want qualified applicants and advertising fully remote positions attracts all walks of life. Companies are so against remote work but most office type jobs can be done remotely
top_arguments.author: Patient_Ad_2357

top_arguments.body: The start of covid when they sent us all home, they required we be on camera at all times if we were working. Everyone could see you and you could see everyone. There were a few times we caught people naked because there home office was in their bedroom and forgot to turn off the camera for lunch. Felt bad for the old ladies. This went on for months until a new CEO was picked. I left that place.
top_arguments.author: Moist-Leg-2539

top_arguments.body: Yep. 40 to 60 mins commute just to sit on Zoom all day with people who aren’t even in the building. No meetings no collaboration just headphones and pretending this makes sense. Feels less like RTO and more like ‘please justify the lease.’ You’re definitely not alone.
top_arguments.author: LabbyinRush

top_arguments.body: temp gigs aren't too bad if you can turn them into something permanent. got to start somewhere, and remote is a win. keep building those interviews.
top_arguments.author: Nice-Championship888

top_arguments.body: Congratulations! I just stared a fully remote full time job this week
top_arguments.author: babydollrecord17

top_arguments.body: I drive to my office to sit at a desk and answer Teams messages and reply to emails, all sent by people at other desks in the same office. I go days at a time without having a face-to-face, work related conversation. American work culture is a mental illness.
top_arguments.author: jaybeau1979

top_arguments.body: No. Assuming you're in the US they could just fire you instead. But I would work with your new boss, not HR. They're the one that has motivation to actually fix this.
top_arguments.author: bduddy

top_arguments.body:
Market is horrible, landing a job is miraculous enough, keep what you have.

Just discuss collectively what the team thinks of meeting just once a month and push for that.
top_arguments.author: stormblaz

top_arguments.body:
For some godforsaken reason upper managers and CEOs love open floor plans. They are the absolute pits.

I look back on the days when I had an actual cubicle as a lost golden age.
top_arguments.author: sabrinajestar

top_arguments.body: I'm rolling at the idea of a bunch old ladies all going "whelp, time for lunch" and IMMEDIATELY getting naked
top_arguments.author: CatsGambit

top_arguments.body: I have a job interview on Tuesday, which will be fully remote and 40 hrs a week. Wish me luck Redditors.
top_arguments.author: ZainMunawari

top_arguments.body:
I'll take this any day over the jobs advertising as remote and then finding out they mean only fridays are remote.

Thinking about it though they're likely screwing themselves out of the top tier candidates that know their worth and only apply to remote roles because they are in demand.
top_arguments.author: icecappmedi

top_arguments.body: Similar to me. Drive 3 days into office, about a 30-40 minute drive each way. Most days I don't have in person meetings, so I'm just wasting time, gas and energy for no real reason other than I have to do it to get my paycheck.
top_arguments.author: DatingAdviceGiver101

top_arguments.body:
> I’m sure the software can’t tell whether I’d I’m scrolling on my phone distracted or if I’m taking a bathroom break. Which were unlimited the last time I checked. 

Bathroom breaks are unlimited if you want to get fired.
top_arguments.author: SatansLoLHelper

top_arguments.body: It is funny how the world and job market has shifted. In the past, the best way to get qualified applicants was by not limiting your job geographically and instead going remote with it. In theory that is still the case, as the candidate pool would be broader, but it also brings a shit tonne of effort in the recruitment and selection process first.

reference_posts.title: Well, this is a new one for me
I can finally say this- I GOT THE JOB. Fully remote, 40 hrs a week
Why aren’t we fighting for remote work again?

top_arguments.body: sometimes the real difference is your resume + profile alignment, not the cover letter itself
top_arguments.author: Awkward-Chemistry627

top_arguments.body: Same. I have not submitted a cover letter in years. If the application requires one, I’ll sometimes submit a boilerplate letter where I simply change the name of the company. I’ve had one job where the hiring manager asked me to write one for my second round of interviews which I begrudgingly did. But I’m not going to waste time writing a letter that likely won’t be read. I fully understand that that might disqualify me from some positions, but it’s a cost/benefit decision that I’m willing to ma
top_arguments.author: Charming-Ebb-1981

top_arguments.body: I looked for “Hi, I read the job description, I’ve looked at your company, and I think I’d be an asset due to X, Y, and Z that are relevant to the job in these ways”. It can be done in a single paragraph.
top_arguments.author: Then-Yam-2266

top_arguments.body: I'm in academia, which is sort of a weird niche industry. In every hiring committee I've ever participated in, including for staff and administrator roles, we've taken cover letters and quality of writing seriously. I know at least one hiring manager who is screening candidates based on obviously AI-generated resumes and cover letters. It's so industry/institution-specific.
top_arguments.author: Dr_Spiders

top_arguments.body:
ChatGPT and I have a pretty good middle of a cover that gets juiced up by trying to cram in job ad key words. But I’ll then take 3 minutes to write the introduction with a reference to the company and their accomplishments and the conclusion with a personal anecdote about an experience not otherwise referenced in my resume to emphasize a soft skill or character trait of mine that would me valuable. 

And to go to your point - half of the companies that I’ve gotten interviews with have mentioned
top_arguments.author: shastapete

top_arguments.body:
I don't bother. Back when I did write them, never had a single person mention that it existed or referenced anything mentioned in it.

So, I stopped submitting them. Changed nothing.
top_arguments.author: principium_est

top_arguments.body:
Best way to stand out is using Wingdings. 

Make your resume hard to decipher and the recruiter winds up working for you! /s
top_arguments.author: Chrono_Convoy

top_arguments.body: These are mostly useless today and if people are submitting them they just change the headliner or chatgpt a quick bullet about the firm.
top_arguments.author: fawningandconning

top_arguments.body: Information out there is so contradictory these days...I just had a hiring manager swear a cover letter is the only way to stand out and get an interview but it also kind of seems like they just want their ass kissed.
top_arguments.author: Innoculous_Lox66

top_arguments.body: nobody writes them. if a job application requires one i either put a . in the text box so the form lets me continue, or i upload my cv again to the required filespace on the form. i've never written a cover letter for any job i've been hired for and i work in data science so it's not like i'm doing unskilled labour or something
top_arguments.author: tiny-but-spicy

top_arguments.body: Most people don't. They use a template and slightly edit it for each company.
top_arguments.author: Batetrick_Patman

top_arguments.body: Hell, mine just needed to change the company name in two places, and the job title in two others. Making a different cover letter for every single job application is exhausting… Hell, I didn’t even bother doing that with my resume; I only had the one.
top_arguments.author: jBlairTech

top_arguments.body:
I'll be honest, I don't read them. 

I also do all of my own screening and scheduling of interviews though and tell HR not to make cover letters a requirement. 

I'll get what I need from the resume.
top_arguments.author: Next_Engineer_8230

top_arguments.body:
I don't write a cover letter unless the company requests it from me directly. In this way, I will know if they are moving forward with me or me or not.

Writing cover letters is a waste of time most of the time. The few companies that do filter you in pre-screening for not submitting a cover letter are companies you don't want to work for anyway.

top_arguments.body:
It’s not a jab it’s a joke. 

Ease up
